key_departures.impact: Traded to Titans in exchange for NT T'Vondre Sweat (Feb 26). Johnson was the Jets' highest-upside edge rusher and a first-round pick (2022). His departure created a significant EDGE hole, though the Jets immediately began filling it via free agency.

Signed 3yr/$42M deal with rival Patriots. AVT was a former first-round pick (No. 14 overall, 2021) who missed all of 2025 with a torn triceps. He reportedly did not want to return to the Jets. Combined with Simpson's departure, both starting guards are now gone, leaving a massive hole at IOL — particularly left guard.

Signed 3yr/$30M deal with Ravens. Started all 34 games at left guard over two seasons in NY. Ranked 4th among LGs with a 77.3 PFF grade in 2024 before declining to 56.9 in 2025. His departure alongside AVT leaves the Jets with no proven starting guards.

Signed 2yr/$17M deal with Browns. Former All-Pro (2023) who regressed significantly in 2025. Was not expected back given scheme fit concerns under Aaron Glenn. Departure partially offset by Demario Davis signing.

Jets wanted to re-sign him but lost him to a 2yr deal with the Falcons. Folk led the NFL in FG% in each of the last three seasons (96.3% since 2023). His departure leaves the Jets needing a new kicker.
Suffered season-ending injury in Week 8 of 2025. Not expected to return. His departure is part of a larger safety overhaul already addressed via the Fitzpatrick trade.
Starter at safety who is not expected to return. Departure addressed by Fitzpatrick trade and Belton signing.

contract.notes: Acquired via trade (Feb 26) for EDGE Jermaine Johnson. Contract details on existing rookie deal not fully disclosed; trade compensation was Johnson straight-up. No new contract extension reported.

signings.analysis: Massive nose tackle (6-4, 340 lbs) acquired via trade for Jermaine Johnson. Sweat becomes the anchor of Aaron Glenn's new 3-4 defensive front. His presence as a true NT paired with Onyemata, Briggs, and Harrison Phillips gives the Jets a deep and credible interior. IDL need fully addressed. Note: draft capital implication — Jets sent a starting edge rusher to acquire an IDL piece, which then escalated EDGE need.

contract.notes: Acquired via trade (7th-round pick from Chargers sent to Miami). AAV of $13.3M ranks 13th at the position per OTC. Full guarantee structure not yet reported; Spotrac shows $15.6M cap hit in 2026.

signings.analysis: Five-time Pro Bowl, three-time All-Pro safety acquired for a 7th-round pick — an extraordinary return. Fitzpatrick (29) had an 81.8 PFF grade in 2025 and reunites with DC Brian Duker. The 3yr/$40M extension is mid-market for a safety with his résumé. His 21 career interceptions are exactly what a Jets secondary that went 0 INTs in 2025 needs. S goes from a tier-1 critical need to addressed. The Jets still need a starting SS2 partner, but the biggest hole is filled.

contract.notes: Trade (6th-round pick sent, 7th-round pick received back). Jets pay only $3.3M of Smith's $19.5M total salary; Raiders cover $16.2M. Effectively a near-minimum cap hit for the Jets.

signings.analysis: The Jets acquired Smith cheaply — paying just $3.3M against the cap — to serve as a 2026 bridge starter. He had a dreadful 2025 (55 sacks, 17 INTs in Las Vegas), but returns to a better supporting cast and OC Frank Reich. This is a 1-year bridge arrangement, not a franchise solution. The Jets still hold the No. 2 overall pick and are widely expected to draft a QB in April. QB remains a long-term need but the 2026 starting position is stabilized. One-year deal structure signals this is not the answer.

Three-year deal with $22.5M fully guaranteed — a real commitment. Ossai, 25, is ascending: 5.0 sacks in each of the last two seasons, career-high 43 pressures and career-best QB pressure % in 2025. He projects as a DE in Glenn's 3-4 front. The 3yr/$36M ($12M AAV) is mid-market EDGE money — below top-of-market ($25M+) but with real guarantees indicating starter investment. EDGE drops from tier-1 critical to tier-2 given Ossai + Enagbare + Sweat + McDonald IV, but the Jets are still likely to select EDGE at No. 2 overall — they remain pass-rush hungry.

contract.notes: Guarantee amount not reported at time of publication.

signings.analysis: 1-year/$10M deal signals a prove-it/rotational role. Enagbare, 26, had 11.5 career sacks over 4 years in Green Bay but projects as a run-defending edge setter. His pass-rush win rate was a career low in 2025. Good depth signing that adds three-down versatility alongside Ossai, but the 1-year structure means this is depth, not a solution. EDGE remains a draft priority.

Two-year/$22M with $15M fully guaranteed — genuine commitment for a 37-year-old. Davis posted an 81.4 PFF grade and elite 88.9 run-defense grade in 2025. HC Aaron Glenn coached him for three years in New Orleans and calls him 'one of the best leaders I've ever been around.' Davis will assume the green-dot (play-caller) role. The age (37) and declining pass-coverage metrics mean this is a 1-2 year bridge, not a long-term fix. LB need drops from tier 1 to tier 3 for 2026, but the team should still add a developmental LB in the draft given Davis's age.

1yr/$10.5M with nearly fully guaranteed money — strong short-term investment at IDL. Onyemata (6-4, 310 lbs) is another big run-stuffer to pair with T'Vondre Sweat. Had a solid 2025 with Atlanta. Signing at a position already addressed by the Sweat trade; this deepens IDL rather than addresses a primary need. The Jets' interior is now genuinely deep: Sweat + Onyemata + Briggs + Harrison Phillips.

contract.notes: Per Ian Rapoport, max value of $5.5M — fully guaranteed base per reports.

signings.analysis: Wright had a breakout 2025 — 5 INTs (tied for league lead among CBs), 11 PBUs, Pro Bowl selection. He fills a critical need as the Jets were the first team in NFL history to go 0 INTs for a full season. However, the 1-year/$5.5M structure screams prove-it deal — the market viewed his 2025 as potentially a one-year wonder. He graded just 47th at CB per PFF. He'll compete with Brandon Stephens as a starter. CB need is partially addressed — still a priority in the draft, especially at corner2/nickel.

contract.notes: Max value up to $6M per reports.

signings.analysis: 1-year/up to $6M is depth-level money. Belton, 25, is a standout tackler (95% efficiency, 4th among safeties in 2025) with 6 career INTs. He'll compete for the SS2 role alongside Malachi Moore. Good value signing that further deepens the safety room post-Fitzpatrick acquisition, but the 1-year structure means S still needs a long-term partner for Fitzpatrick via the draft.

Re-signing of FB Andrew Beck at league-minimum level. OC Frank Reich has clear plans for a fullback in his offense. Fully guaranteed signals he'll have a role, but this is a specialist signing. No impact on team needs.

contract.notes: Re-signed; contract terms not disclosed. Woods (6-7, 253 lbs) had one catch in 4 games in 2025 after joining off waivers. Former OC Frank Reich's Colts drafted him in 2022.

signings.analysis: Retained via re-sign but details undisclosed. Given limited 2025 production (4 games, 1 catch), this is depth/developmental signing. TE remains a need — Jeremy Ruckert and Woods are not proven starters. Frank Reich's scheme values TEs heavily.

contract.notes: Exclusive rights tender — league minimum. Cannot negotiate with other teams.

signings.analysis: ERFA tender locks in a defensive bright spot for league minimum. Briggs had 4 sacks and appeared in all 17 games in 2025. Expected to be a key part of the IDL rotation behind Sweat and Onyemata. No cap impact; no need impact — just good roster management.
Special teams captain retained at minimum. Appeared in only 5 games in 2025 due to injury. Depth/special teams piece only — no impact on LB starter need.

contract.notes: Non-exclusive franchise tag. Jets and Hall have until July 15 to negotiate long-term deal. Hall can negotiate with other teams; Jets retain right of first refusal plus two 1st-round picks if they don't match.

signings.analysis: Franchise tag retains the Jets' best offensive player for 2026. Hall ran for a career-high 1,065 yards in 2025 — the first Jet to hit 1,000 rush yards since 2015. The tag is 4th-highest RB salary in the NFL. Long-term deal negotiations continue through July 15. RB is not a draft need — Hall's presence handles it for 2026 regardless of extension outcome.

recommended_need_adjustments.reasoning: Minkah Fitzpatrick (3yr/$40M, trade) is a 5x Pro Bowl, 3x All-Pro safety — a genuine elite acquisition at near-bargain price. Dane Belton adds depth. S drops from critical need to luxury/developmental pick. Jets may still draft a safety in rounds 3-5 to pair long-term with Fitzpatrick given Belton's 1-year deal.

T'Vondre Sweat (trade, starter NT) + David Onyemata (1yr/$10.5M, starter DT) + Jowon Briggs (ERFA, rotational) + Harrison Phillips gives the Jets genuine interior depth. IDL is no longer a meaningful draft need in 2026.

Franchise tag on Breece Hall ($14.293M fully guaranteed) locks in the Jets' best offensive player. Even if no long-term deal is struck by July 15, Hall is under contract for 2026. RB is not a draft priority.

Joseph Ossai (3yr/$36M, $22.5M gtd) is a legitimate starter-investment at EDGE. Enagbare adds depth. However, with Jermaine Johnson traded away and the Jets recording second-fewest sacks in the NFL in 2025, pass rush remains a critical need. Ossai has only 5 sacks/year and was a 3rd-round pick. This roster still needs an elite edge rusher — draft remains the primary path (the Jets hold the No. 2 pick). Drops from tier-1 to tier-2.

Demario Davis (2yr/$22M, $15M gtd) fills the starter role and takes the green dot. However, he is 37 years old with declining pass coverage. Williams' departure is essentially replaced by Davis, but at 37 the Jets need a developmental LB drafted in rounds 3-5 to ensure continuity beyond 2027.

Nahshon Wright (1yr/$5.5M) is a one-year prove-it signing despite his Pro Bowl 2025 — cannot be counted on as a long-term solution. The Jets still need a CB2/nickel with legitimate commitment. CB remains a tier-2 need for the draft; the Jets have Brandon Stephens, Wright, and Azareye'h Thomas but no proven CB1 on a multi-year deal.

Geno Smith (trade, Jets pay ~$3.3M) provides a viable 2026 bridge starter but is 35 and coming off a dreadful season. This does not address the franchise QB need. The Jets hold the No. 2 overall pick and are widely expected to draft a QB in April. QB remains a tier-2 draft priority — the draft, not FA, is the correct path here, and the Geno acquisition only papers over 2026 while the franchise QB search continues.

ESCALATED. Both starting guards departed: John Simpson (3yr/$30M to Ravens) and Alijah Vera-Tucker (3yr/$42M to Patriots). The Jets have not replaced either. IOL is now the most unaddressed roster hole entering the draft. Josh Myers (ranked 33rd of 34 centers in 2025 per PFF) further compounds the concern at center. IOL upgrades through free agency and/or the draft are urgently needed — this should be a top-3 draft priority.

No signings at WR. Garrett Wilson remains the only proven weapon. The Jets did not address WR in the first wave of free agency and the room remains thin behind Wilson. WR need unchanged — still a priority in rounds 2-4 of the draft.

Jelani Woods re-signed but contract undisclosed and production was minimal (4 games, 1 catch). Jeremy Ruckert is the incumbent. Frank Reich's offense values TE heavily. This position has been minimally addressed and remains a need in rounds 3-5.

NEW NEED CREATED. Nick Folk (36-of-41 FG in 2025, 96.3% accuracy over last 3 seasons) lost to Atlanta on a 2yr deal. The Jets now have no kicker under contract. With one of the most accurate kickers in NFL history departing, this is a legitimate need. They'll need to sign a veteran or find one in the draft.

key_additions.headline: 5x Pro Bowl, 3x All-Pro safety acquired for a 7th-round pick — immediately becomes the anchor of a Jets secondary that went 0 INTs in 2025
Young, ascending edge rusher (career-high 43 pressures in 2025) committed as a starter in Glenn's 3-4 front
Veteran LB reunites with HC Aaron Glenn for 3rd stint — will assume green-dot play-calling duties and stabilize a linebacking corps that lost Quincy Williams
Massive NT (6-4, 340) acquired via trade to anchor Glenn's new 3-4 base defense at nose tackle
Full-circle return: Jets' 2013 2nd-round pick comes back as 2026 bridge starter at near-minimum cap cost while franchise QB hunt continues via the draft
Big-bodied run-stuffer pairs with T'Vondre Sweat to give Jets a formidable interior tandem
Pro Bowl corner (5 INTs in 2025, tied for league lead) brings much-needed playmaking to a secondary that went 0 INTs all season
Run-defending edge setter adds three-down depth to a revamped Jets pass-rush group
Tackling machine (95% efficiency, 4th among safeties) competes for SS2 role alongside Malachi Moore

key_additions.contract_summary: Franchise tag ($14.293M; long-term talks ongoing through July 15)
key_additions.headline: First Jet to rush for 1,000 yards since 2015 retained via franchise tag as the engine of Frank Reich's new offense

key_losses.headline: Former 1st-round pick signs with rival Patriots — leaves the Jets without a proven starter at left guard alongside Simpson's departure
Starting LG for 2 seasons departs; combined with AVT loss, Jets now have no proven starting guards entering the draft
First-round edge rusher dealt to Titans in exchange for NT T'Vondre Sweat — position shift from EDGE to IDL in the Jets' defensive rebuild
Former All-Pro departs after declining 2025 season; replaced by Demario Davis reunion
NFL's most accurate kicker over last 3 seasons (96.3%) walks out the door — Jets now need a new kicker for first time since 2019
